key_quotes: Mine looked different from the other reply. Second floor room directly above the buildings main entrance.
Greenfield itself is kind of situated around a bitch of a hill (affectionate), so depending on where the place is, you may have a short but steep trek
it doesn't get recommended as often as places like Shadyside, or Bloomfield, or Garfield, or Friendship because it doesn't have the same density or as bustling a neighborhood
key_points: Urban campus scatters students across Pittsburgh neighborhoods, no central quad
Ruskin Hall is a known dorm option with variable room configs
Greenfield is cheaper but notably more isolated than Shadyside or Bloomfield

key_quotes: Serving is great. I do it and make enough cash for my bills working only the weekends, but I also work Downtown. I don't know how it would be in Oakland, but I'd look for restaurants in Shadyside
I tend to have a sort of 'alternative' personality, and I feel like I would fit in well in Portland. I like Pitt, but the atmosphere doesn't seem to meld well with my personality.
trying to get into chevron with a foot of snow on one side and a throng of people hogging the sidewalk on the other was genuinely painful
key_points: Pittsburgh's neighborhoods offer strong bars, restaurants, and city social life
Social life extends citywide rather than staying contained on campus
Oakland sidewalk crowding is a frequent daily frustration for students

key_quotes: I worked at the milkshake factory and made like no money lol, work somewhere where you make decent cash like serving in shadyside
I worked front desk at a hotel and I made great cash and got a meal during each shift
There is Giant Eagle (not the best, but nearby)
key_points: Students rely heavily on Oakland and nearby neighborhood restaurants
Almost no campus dining discussion, suggesting low satisfaction or relevance
Surrounding food options are plentiful but off-campus dining dominates

key_quotes: I've been really overwhelmed and honestly depressed. I've been struggling mentally, and yeah, I've had suicidal thoughts.
I'm slightly depressive and my parents are paying out of pocket for my university. I'm at risk of losing my scholarship
I hate the cold weather...I'm not really as happy as I should be here. I want to transfer
key_points: Mental health resources rarely mentioned, suggesting low visibility on campus
One transfer student reported depression and suicidal thoughts amid financial stress
A struggling student's concerns went unaddressed by the institution

key_quotes: A lot of people lost it after 2nd year because of their parents' income changing
my EFC is zero and they are only covering 16k in total and that leaves another 16k a year for me to figure out how to pay
Pitt gave me $30k in aid that I've already accepted, so I have that in the bag. However, I still have to pay $16k for the whole school year
key_points: Need-based aid often disappears after year two when family income shifts
Out-of-state total COA hits roughly $50k with uncertain renewability
In-state students still face around $30k per year in costs

key_quotes: The number of people that fail Calc 1 is....a whole thing.
Jackie is great for Orgo, it's a hard class but you'll be set up really well for it.
I'm getting straight-A's and I feel as though I am not intellectually challenged here... My professor graded a paper that I submitted and told me she'd never seen work like it from a freshman
key_points: Pre-med track is strong with real UPMC clinical and research access
Calculus failure rates are high; ALEKS placement exam is taken seriously
Some students feel under-challenged in early gen-ed coursework

key_quotes: Talk to your advisor. Your advisor can usually help you get into classes for your first semester when you initially transfer.
I emailed pitt asking them if the aid is for all 4 years. They replied telling me it's for the first year.
I applied to Pitt around the 15th of November, but only got my acceptance a couple days ago (mid-March)
key_points: Rolling admissions is praised for reducing application stress early
Financial aid communication requires repeated emails to get clear answers
Advising is helpful for transfer students navigating registration

key_quotes: Pittsburgh is a great small city, and Pitt is a great school
Parking on campus is nearly impossible for classes
Your bus pass should be enough to get to know the areas outside of Oakland and a bike+your feet is more than enough for getting around campus
key_points: Free student bus passes are a repeatedly praised quality-of-life perk
Pittsburgh is consistently called underrated, livable, and neighborhood-rich
Parking is nearly impossible; urban spread can feel disorienting

key_quotes: good GPAs and plenty of internships/co-ops are readily available if you are responsible
I'm a freshman at the University of Pittsburgh...what are the chances that I could get an IB internship even though Pitt isn't a target school?
The whole city of Pittsburgh is geared a little bit towards medicine in a big way
key_points: UPMC ties give health science students built-in clinical experience advantages
Engineering and CS students report solid co-op placement around 77%
Pitt is a non-target for investment banking; finance students are warned

key_quotes: I got a full ride to University of Pittsburgh... Yay
Neither school is worth that kind of debt, especially for pre-med
Pennsylvania sucks. You either spend $30,000 per year as a resident to attend Pittsburgh... or you spend a reasonable amount (~$15,000) and attend one of the awful PASSHE schools
key_points: In-state students with scholarships get strong research university value
Out-of-state students face steep costs with unpredictable aid renewability
Advisors and students consistently warn against heavy debt for Pitt

key_quotes: Carnegie Mellon's little sibling, seems to be ignored by all the 'big kids.' Little do they know, Pitt is secretly a genius, probably planning world dominance.
It's pitt not yale lol, pitt is just a better state school. Relax
I like Pitt, but the atmosphere doesn't seem to meld well with my personality. Also, the snow...
key_points: Pitt skews practical and pre-professional, not a traditional party school
Seen as an underrated public research university, strong but not flashy
Urban setting and UPMC ties drive loyalty among satisfied students

key_quotes: A lot of people lost it after 2nd year because of their parents' income changing

key_points: Financial aid: need-based aid lost after year one with little warning
Course availability: popular STEM classes fill fast, locking out late students
Overcrowding: Oakland pedestrian congestion signals strain on campus infrastructure

key_quotes: They have good relationships with local EMS agencies and hospitals. One semester you will do city hospital and suburban EMS, then the next you will do suburban hospital and city EMS.
The best decision I made during the college admission cycle was applying to a school that does rolling admissions... I knew that I would get to go to a decent college at the end of the day.
You literally bike through Schenley park and you can get from Greenfield to Oakland in ten minutes or less
key_points: Free Port Authority bus pass is a major underadvertised quality-of-life perk
Emergency Medicine BS program offers uniquely strong real clinical placements
Rolling admissions provides low-stress early acceptance, easing application cycle anxiety
